title: My favorite Tool for Solo RPGs - A Deck of Cards (A Land in Peril) (Solo RPG)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OD8Jm9Yk0WY

Daniel (@dungeondive) settles in for a long, cozy chat about his single favourite solo-gaming tool — the humble deck of cards — and tours his sizeable collection, threading in development notes on his own in-progress game, A Land in Peril. He'd rather call the hobby "free-form adventure gaming" than solo RPG, and designs his game (ABCD stats — athletics, brawn, concentration, determination — a d6 dice pool, keyword-built weapons, and no character death) to be system-agnostic so existing tools all plug in.

The collection sprawl is the real content: Mörk Borg's lavish Portents of the De-Gloved Hand and endless-demon decks, the Call to Adventure tarot for character paths, Pocket Lands map cards, Inkwell's prolific side-quest and NPC decks, Philip Reed's beloved outdoor-encounter decks, GM Assist and encounter-builder decks, the Grey Gnome oracle, and Perplexing Ruins' superb Fallen oracle. He explains his bedrock trick — mapping a standard deck's four suits to NPC/urban/dungeon/wilderness sub-tables, face cards as bigger events.

Throughout, he runs little A Land in Peril vignettes — dodging cursed fairies, sidestepping caltrops, navigating crumbling ruins — spending "determination" to narrate outcomes. A warm, browseable inventory of the soloist's toolbox.

----

Standard deck or purpose-built oracle deck — which earns more table time for you, and how do you map the four suits in your own games?
